title: Listening - Part 3: The Mind of a Champion
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a sports psychologist, Libby, and a retired Olympic athlete, Alfie, about the mental challenges of professional sports. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding mental resilience, the pressure of public expectation, and life after retirement.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We often focus on the physical training of athletes, but today we’re looking at what happens between the ears. I’m joined by Dr. Libby Vance, a sports psychologist, and Alfie, a former world-champion sprinter. Alfie, people see the medals, but they don't see the 'mental weight.' What was the hardest part for you?

Alfie: [15] It wasn't the fear of losing; it was the fear of what losing would do to my identity. When you’ve been 'the fast guy' since you were ten years old, your entire self-worth is tied to a stopwatch. [/15] If you have a bad race, you don't just feel like a bad athlete; you feel like a failed human being. The pressure of public expectation is a heavy coat to wear, especially when social media allows everyone to critique your every move in real-time.

Interviewer: Dr. Vance, you work with young athletes. Is this 'identity fusion' becoming more common?

Libby: [16] It’s a huge problem. We see 'early specialization' where kids are treated like professionals at age twelve. They miss out on developing other parts of their personality. [/16] My job is to help them realize that 'athlete' is just their job, not their soul. We use 'psychological flexibility' training—teaching them to accept negative thoughts without being controlled by them. If an athlete can’t handle the 'voice of doubt' in their head during a final, their physical talent won't save them.

Interviewer: Alfie, you’ve talked about the 'dark side' of the flow state. What do you mean?

Alfie: [17] The 'flow' is incredible—it’s like the world slows down and you’re perfectly in tune with your body. But it’s addictive. Nothing in 'normal' life compares to the adrenaline of an Olympic final. [/17] When you retire at thirty, you spend the next forty years looking for that feeling again, and you usually can't find it. It’s why so many former pros struggle with depression or risky behaviors. We’re adrenaline junkies with nowhere to go.

Interviewer: Dr. Vance, how can teams better support athletes through this transition?

Libby: [18] We need to stop seeing 'mental health' as a sign of weakness. In the past, if a player saw a psychologist, they were 'broken.' Now, we try to frame it as 'mental conditioning'—just like lifting weights for your brain. [/18] We also need 'exit programs' that start years before retirement. Athletes need to be encouraged to have hobbies, businesses, or education that exists outside the stadium. They need a 'Plan B' for their ego, not just their bank account.

Interviewer: Alfie, does the modern focus on 'marginal gains'—measuring every calorie and every second of sleep—make the mental strain worse?

Alfie: [19] It can turn you into a robot. When your life is a series of data points, you lose the joy of the sport. You start to feel like a piece of equipment being managed, rather than a person playing a game. [/19] I’ve seen great talents quit because they couldn't stand the constant surveillance of their own bodies.

Interviewer: Final question, Dr. Vance. What is the one quality that separates the 'great' from the 'good'?

Libby: [20] It’s the ability to 'reframing' pressure. The 'good' athlete sees anxiety as a threat that will stop them. The 'great' athlete sees that same racing heart and sweaty palms as their body getting ready for battle. They interpret the fear as 'excitement.' It’s a subtle shift in narrative, but it makes all the difference. [/20]

Interviewer: Alfie, Libby, thank you.

questions.question: Alfie says the hardest part of his career was
options: A) the intense physical pain of daily training.
B) the risk of not being able to earn a living if he lost.
C) the way his sense of self was entirely dependent on his results.
D) the negative comments he received on social media.
questions.explanation: He explains that as 'the fast guy', his self-worth was tied to the stopwatch and losing made him feel like a 'failed human'.

questions.question: Dr. Vance believes that 'early specialization' in sports is harmful because
options: A) children are more likely to suffer serious physical injuries.
B) it prevents young people from developing a well-rounded identity.
C) most kids do not actually have the talent to become professionals.
D) it makes parents too involved in their children's daily lives.
questions.explanation: She notes that treated as pros at twelve, kids miss out on developing other parts of their personality.

questions.question: What does Alfie identify as the 'dark side' of the flow state?
options: A) It makes it difficult for athletes to concentrate on their training.
B) It can lead to dangerous overconfidence during a race.
C) The thrill it provides is impossible to replicate in everyday life.
D) It only happens during competitions, not during practice.
questions.explanation: He describes it as 'addictive' and says that after retirement, you spend forty years looking for that feeling again.

questions.question: Dr. Vance suggests that teams should view psychological support as
options: A) a way to repair players who are mentally ill.
B) an optional extra for those who want it.
C) a proactive form of brain training.
D) a method for identifying which players to fire.
questions.explanation: She says they frame it as 'mental conditioning'—just like lifting weights for your brain.

questions.question: How does Alfie feel about the use of 'data points' in modern sports?
options: A) It helps athletes understand their bodies better than ever before.
B) It can make a sport feel more like a mechanical process than a game.
C) It is the only way to compete at the highest international level.
D) It encourages athletes to be more honest about their health.
questions.explanation: He says it can turn you into a robot and make you feel like 'a piece of equipment' rather than a person.

questions.question: In her final point, Dr. Vance argues that top athletes
options: A) never experience any form of performance anxiety.
B) have naturally lower heart rates during stressful events.
C) view the physical symptoms of stress as a positive sign.
D) use meditation to completely remove the feeling of fear.
questions.explanation: She says they reframe pressure, seeing racing heart and sweaty palms as their body getting ready for battle.
